speaker: interviewer
text: --5. Aaron, we left off talking about Israel, and your parents staying in Israel for 14 years--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, approximately, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --13, 14 years, I believe. Tell me about your, your life there. What happened during those years? You were married in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met an American lady which had joined the kibbutz adjoining to us. And we just fell in love. And I didn't speak English. She didn't-- she hardly spoke Hebrew. But I guess the, the language of love is universal. So I fell in love with her. And subsequently, we married. She moved into our kibbutz. And I had a very good job at the kibbutz. I was the truck driver of the kibbutz. And-- but then we started having problems in the kibbutz with the girls, meaning that they begin to miscarry. I don't know why, but it was like a-- like an epidemic at that time girls, just to-- got pregnant, miscarried. So [? Peninnah-- ?] which was the Hebrew word of my ex-wife and the mother of my children, she-- of our children-- she conceived twice. And twice, she miscarried.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, when the third time she conceived, I had left the kibbutz. Because she thought maybe the life in the kibbutz, or whatever-- but then she had to come to America for treatment. So I followed her. That's why I came to America. So I left everybody, my friends, my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is-- my wife is an American, was and still is an American. And her family lived here. When her mother found out, God rest her soul, that she was having problems with miscarriages, and when she conceived the third time, immediately, we shipped her out. And at that time, her father was also ill. So it coincided all at the same time. And to be truthful-- this is the only way I could be-- at that time, I thought that we would just send her for treatments, and she would come back. However, as the pregnancy progressed, it, it resulted in some other complications. And she had to be bedridden and under constant care.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's where they sent for me. And that's why I came here. I knew that I would be here for a tough time. I came here-- I had no language, no education, no trade, nothing. Extreme pride-- just like my father, I didn't want to depend on anybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found myself a job. But then I keep getting laid off. And I couldn't understand that. In Israel, you have a job, you have a job, in those days, anyway. You don't get laid off.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here, after the third time they got laid off-- we got laid off, there was a company in Trenton, in which one of our acquaintances was an engineer. And he told us that they were hiring. So I went and applied for a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they hired me there. And it was a factory for outfitting the Navy-- the Navy ships with air conditioning. So it was during the Korean War. And there were about 750, 800 people. And there were no Jews there. I was the only one. There were a couple of-- a few displaced persons worked there. But they were just keeping themselves to the side. And he was a-- alone and, and working. And the management kept an eye on them. And I was picked out of one out of-- out of 750, 800 people. There were six of us that were picked out who were management trainees. And I was in my glory. I was going to school at night. I came to, to the States. The first thing, I went and studied English in New York. Then, the, the minute we moved to New Jersey-- Trenton, New Jersey, I went to high school-- never graduated high school. We stayed there for a year and a half. And I was an excellent student. And they kept an eye on me because they knew I had a family. I was working during the day. At night, I was going to school. On the weekends, I had another job. And my wife was also teaching Hebrew at the local synagogue there, at the temple. So they picked me out. And then all of a sudden, the Korean War stops. And they laid off 400 people. I said, wait a minute, guys, time-out. I want a job that I'll never get laid off.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I became a janitor. And for the past 43 years, I've been a janitor and never got laid off, never missed a day's--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] --work in my life. So, I mean, the-- somebody had to do that work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to Los Angeles. And there was a family of ours in Santa Barbara. They were in this business. And I went, and they taught me how to clean windows. And they told me how to strip a floor. And pretty soon, just like in the Marines, you give a Jewish fellow a grenade and a couple of guns, and he's in business for himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] He does well. So there I was, and hustling and bustling, and, and developed a very nice business for myself. And the, the only thing, the trouble is I'm-- I guess I inherited it from my father-- I'm a workaholic.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, after being married for 20 years to my first wife, I had sold my business successfully. We had moved from very modest surroundings in the Fairfax area. We moved to Brentwood, sent the kids to camp, all the trimmings. But I did not stop working hard. And that's me. Tell me about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we have three children. Aryell is my-- is our oldest. He's very much into Jewish affairs and into Jewish life. He's one of the foremost experts on liturgical music. He is the music director and organist for the past 20 years-- and longer even-- of Sinai Temple. He is one of the foremost organists in the country. He was the president of the organists guild and-- extremely proud of him. And it coincides with the High Holidays. And he performs the services there just magnificent at Sinai Temple. That's where he works. My middle son is more into business he is a self-starter that He just started his own business in construction and insurance renovation. And my daughter--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is David. His name is David Aaron. My oldest's name is Aryell. He's named after a friend of mine in Israel that died during the war. And my daughter is-- her name is Leora. And it's after the kibbutz. And they choose [? Urim ?] Leora. 'Leora' means light to me. [? 'Urim' ?] means light. So there's similarity there. And her name is Leora Tamar. And she's a teacher-- excellent teacher. And she's the only one I always say that has any brains, you know. [LAUGHS] But she had a master's degrees in education and then, then lives in Nevada with our granddaughter and her husband, [? Stephen, ?] who is in construction. So thank God they are there. And we-- I told you that's-- I mean, we have four grandchildren. And our granddaughter, Michele just went to college in Santa Barbara. Our daughter Erica is right here in El Camino High School. And then we have Lara, who is in Nevada, that is seven years old. She is now in her second grade. And then we have-- we got one boy after three girls. [LAUGHS] His name is Jacob-- Jacob Aaron. In the Sephardic tradition, we do believe that you can name a, a offspring over-- after a living relative. So he's Jacob-- my uncle's name, who passed away-- and Aaron, mine. And so those are the children and the grandchildren, God bless them. And they doing-- they're doing well. They're healthy, knock on wood. And I hope I continue to work until I'm 94 as my great-grandfather. And I have a very good expression in Ladino. [SPEAKING LADINO], meaning when the father gives, the father is happy. The children are happy. If the children give, the child is unhappy. The son is unhappy. And the father is unhappy. They both cry. Meaning that one father, he can raise 12 children. But God forbid the children have to raise one father. So-- [LAUGHTER] --I intend to work until I'm 94, as my great-grandfather, and die on my job. He, he worked to the last day of his life. And [? Papuczi, ?] God bless his heart, he was quite a-- quite a-- quite a man. And I hope to follow in his footsteps. And that's basically the family. And their mother, [? Peninnah-- ?] Phillis-- after 20 years of marriage, she gave up on me because I'm a workaholic. I wasn't going to change. Then, for a short time, I married a lovely lady, Sandra. But also, she could not change me. I am a workaholic. And then finally, I am with my present wife, also Sandra. And we've been together now 15 years. She has accepted sort of my [LAUGHS] being a workaholic. [LAUGHS] She lives with it. She's pretty well adjusted to it, a little complaint once in a while. But she's pretty cool. She's cool. And a great lady-- she's a wonderful, wonderful grandmother. The children call her-- unbeknown to me, the grandchildren have a marvelous relationship with her. And she's really their grandmother. I mean, so they consider her, and they talk to her. She talks to these grandkids more than she talks to me, I'll tell you that. [LAUGHTER] And that's about as far as the family. And they are all very well-adjusted kids, thank God. None of them had any problems-- major. Of course, you always have problems, but not any major problems. And I hope to live-- for them to live. And, and the good Lord may bless them with good health, and, and-- so they can prosper and do and be worthwhile citizens and make sure that they are a credit to this great country of ours. So that's about the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you enjoy doing what now?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Aside from working.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a workaholic. [LAUGHS] I enjoy working. Well, I, I was-- I was a-- an avid tennis player. Now, when I was in Israel-- first of all, when I was in Bulgaria, we played a lot of soccer and played volleyball. I always loved sports. Then we went to Israel. And, and I loved horseback riding there, and-- as well as-- we had horses in our kibbutz, too. And we rode horses. And so it was soccer. And it was volleyball. And it was horseback riding and swimming. And then I came to America. And I was an avid tennis player until I tried to be too big of a macho man and tore a rotator-cuff muscle. So now I'm little bit into more of a sedate sport. I'm, I'm playing golf. I'm playing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I love the game. But I play it. So hopefully, I will-- one day, I will be much more proficient than I am right now. But I love the game.

speaker: interviewer
text: After talking about this-- your ordeal--

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --surviving the Holocaust, how has this affected your attitude towards Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Whoa, that is a loaded question. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had great doubts. I had great doubts about our faith. I had great doubts about God. I, I could not believe that this can happen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 6 million Jews to perish. And if you look at our history, our history of Jews is drenched in tears and blood and sorrow. Why? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But then I have to think, on the other side of the coin, too, that I'm very proud to be a Jew. Because I also have seen some great contributions that the Jews have given to humanity in science, in, in, in medicine, in the arts. And so there must be something there. What it is, I don't know. But that in itself-- and survival, survival of, of the Jews in Bulgaria, the the survival of, of Israel at its inception when I was there-- and so being attacked by countless armies and hundreds of thousands against us. And we're just alone. And I can't say that everything was a miracle due to the great bravery of the Jews. No question that was part of it. That-- Israel actually reaffirmed my faith there is something bigger and heavier. And it reaffirmed my faith again in Judaism, and, and, and in God, and in our heritage. I'm extremely proud of it. I'm, I'm glad that our children are very much into it. And I do hope when they see the tape-- and especially our grandchildren-- will take heed of that and to guard this tradition [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very, very zealously. Another thing I must tell you is that-- this another miracle is this, this great country of ours. I think you'll see that I'm wearing the flag. I wore this flag since I shed my window-cleaner's uniform. And I need to tell you that only in this country you can accomplish the things that our people have accomplished here. They live in freedom. They, they, they live in total freedom of movement, freedom of religion, freedom of-- freedom, period. And it bothers me that our people here, at time, take these things from granted. We were at a B'nai B'rith function. And they had a prayer. But they did not recite the Pledge of Allegiance. And I made a big stink about that. Because without the Pledge of Allegiance, you couldn't have given the prayer. And I point it out to the officials about it. And I hope they take care of that. I'm extremely proud of being an American. This is my adopted country. And I love it. I'll do anything for it. I'll die for it. I'm extremely involved with, with, with our Marine scholarship foundation in addition all the Jewish affairs. But I feel extremely proud to, to have known so many fine people in the military. My-- two years ago, I celebrated my birthday aboard the aircraft carrier Five years ago, they gave me down the honor at my Christmas luncheon with the ladies to have a Naval Color Guard to my birthday. So I'm tremendously-- [CRYING] a very [CRYING] deep sense of appreciation for, for our country, and, in particular, for our people-- the freedom they enjoyed and what they've done here. And I, I equate America to a Turkey. During the 16th-- I'm sorry. During the 15th century, when the Jews were expelled and they had no place to go from Spain, and they went to Turkey-- and here, during the pogroms and in the shtetls, and all this. And the Jews came to America. And America embraced them with open arms. And they worked. They sweated in the sweatshops. And they put their kids through, through school. And I equate the same situation. And I think it's a great, great place for, for-- actually, there's a very unique experience. There is no place on earth when you can see so many people who may not be in love with each other, but we certainly tolerate each other. For heaven's sake, Jews can't get along with Arabs. And Irish can't get along with Irish, and down the road. And here, you have a conglomeration of all faiths and races. And I think it's a-- it's another miracle. And this country just keeps going. And I must tell that I can expound on it forever. And I believe in it. And, and by God, if I can make it here, [LAUGHS] anybody can make it, let me tell you. And I resent the fact when people come here, and they don't embrace the language or the customs-- and everything American is wrong, you know. And I'm not criticizing anybody or anything. But I say that I, I went to school. And I studied voice and diction, so I can eliminate my accent. I don't like accent. I wanted to be an American. Here I was-- I was in Bulgaria. I was a Jew there. I came to Israel. I was a Bulgarian. I came to America. I was an Israeli. I went back to Israel. I was an American. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, OK. I'm an American. But then let me talk to an American like an American. So anyway, it was-- it was a hard struggle. But my professor, bless his heart, Professor [? Bargeld-- ?] when I went to, to college here, I took [LAUGHS] voice and diction. He said to me, Cohen, don't waste your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] He said, what I would like you-- and everything I think of him, I bless him. Because what I learned from him is to express myself and to enunciate and to pronounce and to project, and everything. I think of him. God bless you, Dr. [? Bargeld. ?] And all the fine professors, they, they taught me in school here. They were marvelous here. I mean, all the love that I-- was instilled in me about this country was-- I was going to City College here, working as a janitor and at night, going to school in college. This country allows you to do that. They taught me, at their expense, how to study English, how to learn English. I mean, really incredible. I hope that people really get to appreciate how great this country is and what it means to, to live here. And I know that I-- I'm praying-- I do know that I've instilled that love for their country to our kids and to our grandkids. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very, very much for this wonderful testimony. Pleasure meeting you. We have some photographs that I want to get to and show that you can share with us. And we're going to do that right away. Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to thank you very much for your time. And I want to thank the cameraman, Tim, that has done a great job. And I want to thank everybody involved in the Shoah Foundation. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. [SOBBING] And thank you very much. The first one to the left is my aunt Victoria. And the person above the person with the white beard is my grandfather. His name was Bechor, which means first-born, bechor. But his name was really Aaron-- Aaron, just like me. I have his name. His last name is Cohen. And I don't know the rest of the people. The country where it was taken was in Bulgaria. And it was right in Gorna Dzhumaya. And the year it was taken was 1921. Those are the pictures-- this is the picture of my maternal grandparents. And in the middle, it's my cousin [? Lily. ?] And that was in Bulgaria-- Samokov. And it was 1921 when that was taken. And their last name was Cohen, C-O-H-E-N. My mother name and father name both were Cohen. This is my paternal grandmother. And this is in Bulgaria, in Gorna Dzhumaya. And it was in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: Name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Grandmama Cohen. We called our grandparents grandpapa and grandmama. And that's-- I spent only one summer with her and never saw her again. And-- This is my father, [? Moise ?] Moshe [? Musa ?] Cohen. And the country where it was taken-- it was Bulgaria. It was in Gorna Dzhumaya. And the year that it was taken was 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lady on the left is my mother, Ester Cohen. And the lady to the right is her cousin [? Rebekkah. ?] And it was taken in Bulgaria. And that was her high school graduation picture. And she was-- it was taken in 1922. And she was 19 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my cousin. I'm sorry, that's my Uncle Jacques, my favorite uncle. And his name is Jacques, like Jacob, Cohen. And the country in which it was taken was Bulgaria. The city was Plovdiv. And the year it was taken was 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is my Aunt Ester, a, a sister of my brother-- a sister of my father. And to the right is the firstborn of 13 children. And her name is Buka, meaning from 'bechor,' which means firstborn. And it-- those two pictures simultaneously were taken in 1938. The country where they was taken was Bulgaria. And the city that was, was taken-- it was Plovdiv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is myself, A'nrico. In the middle was our Uncle Joshua-- Josuah. It was-- and to the right is my cousin, also A'nrico, who was the son of my Aunt Victoria, the sister of my father and my Uncle Joshua. And it was taken in 1938. And it was in Gorna Dzhumaya, Bulgaria. All their names, the last names, were Cohen. The picture was taken in Plovdiv, Bulgaria, in 1944. Those are three friends together. In the middle is myself, Aaron Cohen. To the right is Meyer Farki. And to the left is Jacob Benveniste. The pictures were taken in Plovdiv, Bulgaria. And the year was 1944. And as you could see, we are wearing the Jewish stars, which we were mandated to whereby the fascist government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of our children. The young lady, her name is Leora Tamar Cohen. And that was her bat mitzvah. It was at 1973. To the left of her is her brother, David Cohen. And in the middle was, God rest his soul, Cantor [? Erstein. ?] And to the right is the oldest, Aryell Cohen. And the place it was taken was the United States of America. And it was in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our granddaughter, Michele. She is 18 years old. And the picture was taken in 1995, Los Angeles, California, United States. This is Erica Cohen. She is 16 years old. That picture was taken this year, 1996. And it was in Los Angeles, in the United States. This is Lara [? Elisse ?] Bennet. She is seven years old. This auto was taken-- this photo was taken last year, 1995. It was taken in Las Vegas, Nevada, United States. This is Jacob Aaron Cohen. And he is two years old. And the picture was taken this year, 1996, San Diego, California, United States. By the time I'm finished, I got-- This is Sandra Ellen Cohen. It's our wedding picture taken in 1983, Bel Air, Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is just a month ago, 1996-- the month of August, 1996. It was at the grave of the King Boris III at the monastery of Rila.
